author: @gangleweed
text: The safest way to transport Hydrogen is in it's liquid form alloyed with oxygen commonly called water.........the internal combustion engine can still be used to burn the hydrogen and the on board batteries supply the power to split the water into Hydrogen and oxygen to be consumed in the engine.......that's so simple even a high school kid can understand it and there is no risk of a fire or an explosion ever........I think about 2 litres of water can be converted to give a range of 300 to 400 KM's of distance even if the efficiency is low.

replies.author: @gangleweed

author: @Rhannmah
text: I think that Ammonia is the most suitable hydrogen carrier as it is easy to store, extremely energy dense and easy to produce. I did some calculations a while ago and i arrived at the conclusion that with current ammonia fuel cells, an electric car could have a range very similar to a gasoline car for the same volume of fuel. Quite interesting!

replies.author: @Rhannmah
replies.text:
@garysmith5025 

Yes ammonia is rather dangerous, but even today it's produced and transported in millions of tons yearly so it's feasible, the infrastructure already exists. I brought up cars as an example everybody can understand, but i'm mostly thinking about vehicle types where batteries can't be used. Planes, cargo ships, stuff like that.

> filling station logistics would be on another level even compared to hydrogen

they would be extremely similar because hydrogen also has to be pressurized and cannot be let to escape either. Also, at least ammonia has a very detectable smell.

>Ammonia is just another fossil fuel industry Trojan Horse.

What? How would it benefit the fossil fuel industry?

author: @billiemfan
text: I am fascinated by liquid gallium melting aluminum whereby adding water gives a rather explosive (eye protection and stand back) end result of hydrogen.  Safe way to store, transport and convert hydrogen?  This tidbit came from the Action Lab youtube vids.  If you've covered this, I truly apologize, I am late to the party but always love your videos that I've seen.  Thanks as always for your dedication to teaching, experimentation and keeping it real.  If you have covered this, I'd love to see it.  If you haven't, I'd still love to see your take on it.

replies.author: @billiemfan
replies.text: OH, I found your video on this topic!  Scary thing is, I had actually watched it.   Wish I'd searched before I posted.  Sincerest apology.

replies.author: @Rhannmah
replies.text: The problem with gallium-infused aluminium hydrogen production is that it's really hard to get all your gallium back once all the aluminium has reacted with water. You always lose some, and it's a messy process because you're left with a bunch of aluminium oxides too, which you have to get rid of somehow.

author: @railgap
text: That's nice, but Hydrogen, as we all know, is just an energy storage medium - it's not a primary source of energy because there are no hydrogen wells. It's a substitute for batteries. We already have good batteries. What we need is more energy to charge them with, energy to crack and pressurize Hydrogen with, and so on. Got any idea for that?

author: @Drjtherrien
text:
The less expensive catalysts are very cool! That said, they work by lowering the activation energy required for a specific reaction to occur but the reaction is brought to completion through the bonding process which does not involve the catalyst. This does not help in reactions that require a net energy input, so electrolysis does not benefit from this.

Still a very cool step forward.

author: @reypolice5231
text: Looking forward to more about this when available. Thank you.

author: @4Nanook
text: Catylists don't lower or increase the net amount of energy produced in a chemical reaction, they just lower the barrier to that reaction.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text:
You are correct.

That said, much of the problem with hydrogen production at small scale is that you need much higher voltages (often ~2V) rather than what the actual electrolysis reaction uses (1.23V), so you end up with a lot of energy as wasted heat. I don't expect these catalysts to do too much to reduce this gap, but maybe they will.

replies.author: @spectrumofreality
replies.text: Reactions don't produce energy...

replies.author: @spectrumofreality
replies.text: @ThisRandomUsername Heat increases the efficiency in electrolysis.

replies.author: @Alkimi
replies.text: ​@spectrumofreality absolutely they do. Exothermic reactions produce heat. Internal combustion engines in cars use the kinetic energy released by the combustion reaction of gasoline. If you mix potassium, calcium, or lithium with water, an enormous amount of energy is released. Oxygen is called Oxygen because of its involvement in the release of energy from matter. (The Ox 🐂 is the energy source that pulls the plow). Remember that matter consists of an amount of energy equal to its mass times the speed of light squared (E=MC²). The energy is released from the matter.

replies.author: @spectrumofreality
replies.text: @Alkimi See the first law of thermodynamics champ. I will repeat, reactions do not produce energy!

author: @Vibe77Guy
text: An inexpensive catalyst for hydrogen fuel cells could revitalize the interest in the Edison NiFe home battery system. Automating the maintenance of adding water and charge to the batteries.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: There are dozens of other methods to produce hydrogen without oil besides electrolysis, for example concentrating solar can use thermochemical water splitting cycles, biomass can use reforming/gasification, electricity from the wind, hydropower, tidal power, ... can use ultrasound or microwaves and many other examples.

replies.author: @Vibe77Guy
replies.text:
@vitordelima 
I'm referring to the recombinant process to produce electricity, not the generation of gas process.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @Vibe77GuyBut the storage of energy as hydrogen would require "splitting" water the same way (or recharging it via other means) and I doubt using the same catalyst as the fuel cell that consumes it would work well. Bizarrely modern NiFe batteries are competitive with Li-ion.

replies.author: @Vibe77Guy
replies.text:
@vitordelima  
The Edison Battery does split water into oxygen and hydrogen naturally in normal operation. It's one of the reasons that water has to be added.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @Vibe77Guy What I understood from your post was that hydrogen could be a replacement for such batteries in a similar product for homes.

author: @jjamespacbell
text:
Hydrogen for ammonia and industry applications where the hydrogen is produced at the point of use is a great application, hydrogen for transportation or burning along with natural gas is a fools errand. To transport the same amount of energy that a diesel tanker can deliver would take 16 hydrogen tube tankers. Hydrogen has now been found to be a greenhouse gas. Hydrogen embrittles other metals and makes any equipment used for production very fragile and in constant need of repair, many hydrogen refill stations are always out of service.
Please do some of your own research on this subject.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: Spam.

replies.author: @BenjaminGoose
replies.text: "Hydrogen has now been found to be a greenhouse gas" Not if you burn it.
